coredata.dc:title: One-pot hydrothermal method synthesis carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor for the sensitive and selective detection of clioquinol
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Qin
An, Xuanxuan
Pan, Shuang
Zhen, Shujun
Hu, Yongmei
Hu, Xiaoli
coredata.dc:description: Clioquinol (CQ) is not only used to treat skin diseases, but also CQ is a potential pharmaceutical for the treatment of neurodegenerative diseases. However, only a few researches report the detection of CQ. Thus, it is of great significance to accurately quantify CQ. Herein, we developed a CQ fluorescent sensor by Cu2+ and carbon dots (CDs). Cu2+ adsorbed on the surface of CDs preventing the non-radiative recombination of the holes in the valence band and the excited electrons in the CDs conduction band, which brings about an increase in the fluorescence intensity of CDs. However, a complex is formed owing to the strong affinity with Cu2+ and CQ causing that Cu2+ is disrobed from the CDs surface and then it quenched the fluorescence of CDs through inner filter effect (IFE). The CDs + Cu2+ system displays preferable sensitivity and high selectivity toward CQ in a range of 40.0–400.0 μmol L−1 with a detection limit of 4.7 μmol L−1. In addition, the strategy is employed for the quantification of CQ in clioquinol cream with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Clioquinol
Carbon dots
Cu2+
Fluorescence sensor
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity of Cu2+ and CQ and interference of other substances on CQ detection
Analysis of CQ in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs. (B) The size distribution histogram of CDs. (C) XRD of CDs. (D) FT-IR spectra of CDs. (E) XPS spectra of CDs. (F) High resolution spectra of C 1S. (G) High resolution spectra of N 1S.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 390 nm. (B) The UV absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs.

The effects of various conditions on the fluorescence intensity of CDs (A) pH (2.0–12.0, 0.04 mol L−1); (B) ionic strengths (NaCl, 0.0–1.0 mol L−1); (C) UV exposure time (0.0–60.0 min); (D) Temperature (10–40 °C); Conditions: V CDs = 25.0 μL, λ ex = 360 nm.

The effect of different parameters on the detection of CQ. (A) pH (2.0–12.0) on fluorescence intensity of CDs + Cu2+ and CDs+Cu2++CQ system. (B) Reaction time (0–60 min) on fluorescence intensity of CDs + Cu2+ system. (C) Reaction time (0–60 min) on fluorescence intensity of CDs + Cu2++CQ system. (D) The concentrations of Cu2+ (0–120.0 μmol L−1). Conditions: V CDs = 25.0 μL, λ ex = 360 nm, c CQ = 150.0 μmol L−1, pH 8.0, c Cu 2+ = 20.0 μmol L−1, Reaction time = 30 min.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs + Cu2+ system in the presence of different concentration of CQ (40.0–400.0 μmol L−1). (B) The linear of ΔF with different concentration of CQ (40.0–400.0 μmol L−1).Calibration curve for CQ. Conditions: V CDs = 25.0 μL, λ ex = 360 nm, pH 8.0, c Cu 2+ = 20.0 μmol L−1, Reaction time = 30 min.

(A)Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, CDs + Cu2+, CDs + CQ and CDs + Cu2++CQ. The final concentrations of Cu2+ and CQ were 20.0 μmol L−1 and 150.0 μmol L−1, respectively. (B) UV absorption spectra of CDs, Cu2+, CQ, CDs + Cu2+, CDs + CQ, Cu2++CQ and CDs + Cu2++CQ. (C) The UV absorption spectra (dash line) of CQ and Cu2++CQ, and the fluorescence excitation, emission spectra (solid line) of CDs. (D) The fluorescence decay curves of CDs and CDs + Cu2++ CQ.

(A) The selectivity of Cu2+. (concentrations of Co2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, K+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Na+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Al3+ and Cu2+ were 20.0 μmol L−1) (B) The selectivity of CQ. (concentrations of Mn+ was 20.0 μmol L−1, concentrations of CQ was 350.0 μmol L−1) (C) Relative fluorescence intensity of CDs + Cu2+ system in the presence of diverse interfering substances. (concentrations of interference substances were 3500.0 μmol L−1, concentrations of CQ was 350.0 μmol L−1).
Schematic illustration of the fluorescence sensing for CQ based on CDs-Cu2+ system.
The different detection methods for CQ.
Detection of CQ in clioquinol cream (n = 5).

head.ce:title: One-pot hydrothermal method synthesis carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor for the sensitive and selective detection of clioquinol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chengdu Second People's Hospital, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610017, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chengdu second people’s hospital, Chengdu, Sichuan 610017, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Clioquinol (CQ) is not only used to treat skin diseases, but also CQ is a potential pharmaceutical for the treatment of neurodegenerative diseases. However, only a few researches report the detection of CQ. Thus, it is of great significance to accurately quantify CQ. Herein, we developed a CQ fluorescent sensor by Cu2+ and carbon dots (CDs). Cu2+ adsorbed on the surface of CDs preventing the non-radiative recombination of the holes in the valence band and the excited electrons in the CDs conduction band, which brings about an increase in the fluorescence intensity of CDs. However, a complex is formed owing to the strong affinity with Cu2+ and CQ causing that Cu2+ is disrobed from the CDs surface and then it quenched the fluorescence of CDs through inner filter effect (IFE). The CDs + Cu2+ system displays preferable sensitivity and high selectivity toward CQ in a range of 40.0–400.0 μmol L−1 with a detection limit of 4.7 μmol L−1. In addition, the strategy is employed for the quantification of CQ in clioquinol cream with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Clioquinol (5-chloro-8-hydroxy-7-iodoquinoline, CQ) is a halogenated hydroxyquioline with anti-fungal actions [1]. CQ is used as creams for the treatment of skin infections initially [1]. In recent years, there have been previous research reports that CQ acting as bioactive metal chelator is a potential substance for treatment prion disease and Alzheimer's disease [2–4]. The strategy is attributed to CQ (a chelating agents) bind specific metal ions (for example copper (II)), which can dissolve the amyloid deposits by effective against preventing metal-α-beta interactions [5–7]. Moreover, CQ is considered as a potential pharmaceutical for the treatment of neurodegenerative diseases and human prostate cancer [8,9]. However, long term or excessive administration of CQ can cause neurotoxicity and induce lesions in the retina and subacute myeloid-optic neuropathy [10–12]. To date, the detection techniques for CQ include thin layer spectroelectrochemistry [13], electrochemical method [14], fluorescent spectrometry [15] and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [16] etc. At the expense of high sensitivity, some of these methods have drawbacks such as complex electrode modification processes, operation processes and derivation processes. Thus, it is eager to explore low cost, fast response, facile sensor to accurately quantify CQ.

Metal ions as regulators of optical sensing, can change the optical properties of optically active nanocrystals when they can be absorbed on nanocrystals or serve as a bridge to connect nanocrystals [17–20]. For instance, through metal-enhanced fluorescence effect, the fluorescence intensity of nanocrystals can be enhanced by the interaction of fluorophores with metallic nanomaterials [21–24], and the nanocrystals aggregation triggered by metal ions [25–28]. Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention and have a broad application prospects of catalysis, analysis, biological labeling, and medicine owing to their unique size, non-toxicity, biological compatibility and environmental-friendly capacity [29–34]. In the field of fluorescence detection, CDs as a fluorescence sensor have been widely reported. For example, synthesized fluorescent CDs can be employed for drugs and biological small molecules determination [35,36]. Furthermore, compared with the purity CDs counterparts, the photophysical properties and stability of metal ion enhanced - CDs can be improved, which has been applied for sensing and PM adsorption properties [26].

To date, only a few researches reported detection of CQ. Herein, using Cu2+ as the mediator, we reported a strategy for CQ detection employing CDs synthesized by ammonium citrate and ammonium thiocyanate as fluorescence sensors (Scheme 1 ). Initially, Cu2+ adding into CDs enhanced the fluorescence intensity of CDs, but the high affinity with CQ and Cu2+ disrobed Cu2+ from the CDs surface and thus quenched the fluorescence because of IFE. Furthermore, the proposed method was successfully applied for CQ detection in real samples.

All the reagents were analytical reagent. All the reagents were used as received without further purification and the water used in the experiments was deionized water. Ammonium citrate and ammonium thiocyanate were purchased from Macklin Biochemical (Shanghai, Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd.). 5-chloro-8-hydroxy-7-iodoquinoline was obtained from Aladdin Biochemical (Shanghai, Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.). Sodium hydroxide, copper sulfate and quinine sulfate were purchased from Oriental Reagent Factory (Sichuan, China). Phosphoric acid, acetic acid and boric acid were supplied by Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China).

The samples were characterized by Hitachi fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan, F-2500), Hitachi UV–vis spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan, UV-2450), infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Tokyo, Japan, FTIR-8400S), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI, Talos, F200X), X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, Thermo Scientific Escalab, USA, 250Xi) and X-Ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker, Germany, AXS-D8).

CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal method according to a previous procedure with some modifications [37]. Briefly, 0.4 g of ammonium citrate and 0.2 g of ammonium thiocyanate were added in 15.0 mL of deionized water. The mixed solutions were heated at 160 °C for 6 h. After the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min to remove large particles. Then, the CDs were obtained using 1000 Da dialysis bags to get rid of small molecules from the supernatant underwent. Finally, the solution to CDs was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The quantum yield (QY) was measured according to the previously reported method [38]. The QY of CDs was determined by quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 (at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, QY of 54%) as reference. The formula used was as follows:

Φ was the QY and F was the integrated fluorescence intensity, s and u were the quinine sulfate and the CDs. A stand for the absorbance at 360 nm. According to the formula, the QY of CDs was calculated to be about 61%.

BR buffer solution (175.0 μL, pH 8.0, 0.04 mol L−1), Cu2+ (25.0 μL, 20.0 μmol L−1) CDs (25.0 μL) and different concentrations of CQ (25.0 μL) were added into the tube. Deionized water was added into the above solution and the final total volume of detection was 1.25 mL. After incubating for 30 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra of the mixing solutions were recorded with the slit widths of 10 nm and the excitation wavelength at 360 nm throughout the experiment.

TEM image (Fig. 1 A) of newly prepared CDs illustrated quasi-spherical morphology and uniform particle size distribution. In addition, CDs displayed an average diameter of 1.72 nm in the range of approximately 0.52 nm–3.70 nm in Fig. 1B. A broad diffraction peak could be observed in the XRD pattern of the CDs (Fig. 1C), which demonstrated the typical disordered graphitized structure of CDs [39]. FT-IR spectra of CDs was illustrated in Fig. 1D. The absorption bands at 3419 cm−1 and 1629 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of O–H and C=O, respectively [40]. The peaks located at 2052 cm−1 and 1407 cm−1 were ascribed to C–N and N–H bending vibration [41,42]. The peak at 634 cm−1 was the bending vibration of the S–O [43]. Fig. 1E showed the XPS spectra of CDs that indicated CDs mainly contained three elements: C, N and O. And the corresponding content of each element was C 83.97%, N 2.79% and O 13.24% (At%). In the high resolution spectra of C 1s (Fig. 1F), the two peaks at 284.8 eV and 285.4 eV belonged to the C–C/C=C and C–O/C–N, respectively [44]. Two nitrogen species appear to include pyridinic N at 400.4 eV and pyrrolic N at 399.2 eV (Fig. 1G), which showed that the N atoms exist in the form of amino groups [45].

Fig. 2 A showed the fluorescence emission peak position of as-prepared CDs at various excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 390 nm. The maximum emission wavelength was all around 440 nm without significant displacement, which demonstrated the surface of the CDs was uniform [38]. In addition, the excitation, emission spectra and UV absorption spectra of CDs were recorded. The maximum excitation wavelength of CDs was 360 nm under maximum emission wavelength at 440 nm as shown in Fig. 2B. Fig. 2B also illustrated there was one strong UV absorption band centered about at 340 nm due to the n → π* transition.

The effects of pH, ionic strengths and UV exposure time on the fluorescence intensity of the as-obtained CDs were studied at room temperature. In Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs increased gradually from pH 2.0 to 5.0 and maintained stable in the range of pH 5.0 to 12.0. Yang et al. studied CDs containing amine, hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups synthesized by similar precursor ammonium citrate and found that the above groups formed hydrogen bonding under acidic conditions [46]. Therefore, when the pH from 5.0 to 2.0, the quenching of CDs fluorescence may be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonding [47]. Fig. 3B showed that fluorescence intensity of CDs was decreased slightly by the increase of UV exposure time, which may be due to the presence of slight photobleaching. The fluorescence intensity had no obvious change as concentration of NaCl increased (Fig. 3C). In addition, thermal stability of CDs was tested at different temperatures. It was clear that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs hardly changes with the increase of temperature (Fig. 3D). To sum up, CDs had stable fluorescence performance and good practical application effect.

In order to improve the detection performance and effectively control experimental interference, we optimized the experimental parameters (pH, reaction time and concentrations of Cu2+). As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs + Cu2+ and CDs+Cu2++CQ system changed within pH range from 2.0 to 12.0 of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer. It showed that pH had significant effect on the above two systems. When pH value was 8.0, the fluorescence intensity difference between CDs + Cu2+ system with CDs+Cu2++CQ system reached maximal value. In addition, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs + Cu2+ and CDs+Cu2++CQ system were almost unchanged after 30 min in Fig. 4B and C. When the concentration of Cu2+ was above 20.0 μmol L−1, the fluorescence intensity remained stable in Fig. 4D. Therefore, pH 8.0, 30 min of reaction time and Cu2+ at 20.0 μmol L−1 concentration were chosen as the optimal experiment conditions for CQ detection.

Fig. 5 A showed the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of Cu2+ decreased gradually with the increasing concentration of CQ under the optimal experiment conditions. Fig. 5B demonstrated a good linear relationship between the concentration of CQ and ΔF (ΔF = F 0 − F 1), where F 0 and F 1 referred to the fluorescence intensity of CDs + Cu2+ system in the absence and presence of CQ, respectively. The linear equation was expressed as ΔF = 10.17 c + 770.94 (R 2 = 0.9980) with the detection limit was 4.7 μmol L−1 according to the 3σ/k (σ was the standard deviation of eleven blank samples, and k was the slope) principle in the linear range of 40.0–400.0 μmol L−1. Table 1 listed a comparison of this work and other reported works for CQ sensing. In terms of materials, compared with synthetic procedure of Sn(MoO4)2 Nanoplates, SQDs and BSA-AuNCs, one-pot hydrothermal method synthesis of CDs greatly simplified the synthetic procedure by avoiding the complicated multi-step preparation. Compared with HPLC and electrochemistry, the instrument operation of this method was simple and the analysis time of this method was relatively short. In addition, although our proposed method was not the most optimal in Table 1 on detection limit and sensitivity, it was no complicated calculation and with a relatively wide linear range. Thus, this method was applicable to assay of CQ and exhibited significant superiorities, such as simple, effective, comparatively low-cost and simple operation.

As described in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescent spectra of CDs were identical to that of the mixture of CDs and CQ, which meant there was no interaction between CDs and CQ. When CDs were mixed with Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity was enhanced effectively. However, adding CQ into the CDs + Cu2+ system, the fluorescence intensity was weakened obviously. The result indicated that the fluorescence of CDs was quenched effectively because of the substance produced by the interaction of Cu2+ and CQ. We had been displayed UV absorption spectra of related system in Fig. 6B. There was no energy transfer between CDs and Cu2+, and the structure of CDs was not destroyed because the position of UV absorption peak (Fig. 6B) and the position of the maximum emission wavelength (Fig. 6A) of CDs were the same as the CDs + Cu2+ system [51,52]. The fluorescence intensity was enhanced effectively adding Cu2+ into CDs (Fig. 6A) because Cu2+ adsorbed and stabilized on the surface of CDs preventing the non-radiative recombination of the holes in the valence band and the excited electrons in the CDs conduction band [53,54]. Moreover, compared with UV absorption spectra of Cu2+ and CQ, a new UV absorption peak appeared in UV absorption spectra of CQ + Cu2+, which indicated the formation of the complex. The complex can effectively inhibit fluorescence intensity (Fig. 6A), and as shown in Fig. 6C the absorption spectra of CQ + Cu2+ overlap partially with the excitation and emission spectra of CDs [55]. Therefore, IFE was considered as possible quenching mechanism. Fluorescence lifetime was an important parameter to prove IFE [55]. Fig. 6D showed the fluorescence attenuation curve of CDs, and CDs + Cu2++CQ system and the average luminescence lifetime were calculated to be 6.87 ns and 6.93 ns, respectively. The result demonstrated the fluorescence lifetime was no significant change. In summary, the fluorescence quenching mechanism was ascribed to IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of Cu2+ and CQ and interference of other substances on CQ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under similar experimental conditions, different ions (Co2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, K+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Na+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Cu2+) were added into CDs. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the relative fluorescence intensity (F 1/F 0) of other inorganic ions was around 1.0 (the value of the dotted line in the figure was equal to 1.0) excepted for Cu2+, indicating that other ions cannot enhance the fluorescence intensity of CDs under the same conditions, where F 0 and F 1 were the fluorescence intensity of CDs without and with relevant ions. The result suggested that Cu2+ could selectively enhance the fluorescence intensity of CDs.

The evaluation of sensor selectivity was crucial to the determination of CQ. Considering CQ reacted with other metal ions to quench the fluorescence of CDs, we investigated the relative fluorescence intensity of CDs + CQ + Mn + system (Fig. 7B). Mn + included Pb2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, K+, Ba2+, Na+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Cu2+. It can be seen that in addition to CDs + CQ + Cu2+ system, other metal ions had no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of this system. Thus, the CDs + Cu2+ system could be served as a fluorescence sensor for CQ detection. In addition, we had been tested a series of the potential interference foreign species (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, glucose, starch, cyclodextrin, sucrose, l-cysteine, arginine, alanine, serine, glycine, histidine, hydrocortisone, betamethasone) to examine the specificity of CDs + Cu2+ system towards CQ (Fig. 7C). The results showed that the CDs + Cu2+ system still had good anti-interference capability even if the concentration of potential interference substance was ten times that of CQ. Therefore, our proposed assay performed specifically recognition and prominently selectivity for CQ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of CQ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrated the feasibility and practicality of this method, clioquinol cream purchased from pharmacy was used as the real samples of the determination of CQ. The cream (2.0 g) was diluted with ethanol (50.0 mL) then filtered through 0.22 μm filter membranes (no need for other cumbersome pretreatment). The filtrate was used to monitor CQ in clioquinol cream using standard addition method. The test result was showed in Table 2 . The recoveries of CQ in real samples were in the range of 98.6%–104.5%, and with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.7%–2.2%, indicating that this method no obvious system error and had a huge potential for determination of CQ in real samples.

In this work, a facile and sensitive fluorescent sensor for CQ determination based on CDs + Cu2+ system was constructed. The proposed fluorescent sensor displayed good selectivity to CQ and then was applied to the determination of CQ in clioquinol cream with satisfactory results. Therefore, the CDs + Cu2+ system for the determination of CQ is of practical value. Moreover, one-pot hydrothermal method synthesis of CDs greatly simplified the synthetic procedure by avoiding the complicated multi-step preparation compared with other methods. The as-obtained CDs produced blue emission of up to 61% quantum yield and had stable fluorescence performance. Therefore, the CDs have broad application prospects in the analysis and detection of target molecules. Attempts to increase the application of the CDs are in progress.

Qin Tan: Conducting experiments, Writing – original draft, preparation. Xuanxuan An: Data curation. Shuang Pan: Investigation. Shujun Zhen: Data curation. Yongmei Hu: Supervision. Xiaoli Hu: Reviewing and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974109) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. XDJK2019TY003).
